blocks.text: You want something and do not have it; so you commit murder. And you covet something and cannot obtain it; so you engage in disputes and conflicts. You do not have, because you do not ask.
2 : , probably hyperbole, although the charge is leveled three times in the letter (see 2.11; 5.6n.).
You ask and do not receive, because you ask wrongly, in order to spend what you get on your pleasures.
3 : , here the problem is the purpose of the request ( ) rather than “doubt” (see 1.6–8).
Adulterers! Do you not know that friendship with the world is enmity with God? Therefore whoever wishes to be a friend of the world becomes an enemy of God.
4 : , Greek is feminine (“Adulteresses!”), recalling prophetic tradition of casting Israel as God's unfaithful wife (Jer 3.6–10; Ezek 16.8–52; Hos 1–3; cf. Mk 8.38). , contrast 2.23.
Or do you suppose that it is for nothing that the scripture says, “God yearns jealously for the spirit that he has made to dwell in us”?
5 : , either a general statement setting up the citation in the next verse or a quotation from an unknown source.
But he gives all the more grace; therefore it says, “God opposes the proud, but gives grace to the humble.”
6 : Quotation from Prov 3.34.
Submit yourselves therefore to God. Resist the devil, and he will flee from you.
Draw near to God, and he will draw near to you. Cleanse your hands, you sinners, and purify your hearts, you double-minded.
8 : , the seat of will and intellect (Mt 13.15; Jn 13.2), and so a correction to that results from nearness with God (cf. 1.7–8).
Lament and mourn and weep. Let your laughter be turned into mourning and your joy into dejection.
Humble yourselves before the Lord, and he will exalt you.
9–10 : Repentance in anticipation of the great reversal at the last day (see 1.9–10).

Do not speak evil against one another, brothers and sisters. Whoever speaks evil against another or judges another, speaks evil against the law and judges the law; but if you judge the law, you are not a doer of the law but a judge.
11 : , the law properly rules over the readers (2.8; cf. 1.25).
There is one lawgiver and judge who is able to save and to destroy. So who, then, are you to judge your neighbor?
4.1–12: Relations with God and others.
12 : God is by changing one's will (1.18, 21) or to by being left to one's “desire … sin … [and] death” (1.14–15).
Come now, you who say, “Today or tomorrow we will go to such and such a town and spend a year there, doing business and making money.”
Yet you do not even know what tomorrow will bring. What is your life? For you are a mist that appears for a little while and then vanishes.
4.14 : , Christ's return “is near” (5.8–9), but the precise time is unknown (Mk 13.32). , the impermanence of this age and its ways of life (1.11).
Instead you ought to say, “If the Lord wishes, we will live and do this or that.”
15 : , actions are properly determined by God's will and word (1.22).
As it is, you boast in your arrogance; all such boasting is evil.
Anyone, then, who knows the right thing to do and fails to do it, commits sin.
4.13–5.9: Coming judgment.
17 : Theologically termed “sins of omission” arising from failing to do what one knows God desires (2.16n.).
